problem_statement: In triangle ABC let M, N, P be the midpoints of BC, CA, AB. Prove that the three medians AM, BN, CP are concurrent (at the centroid G), and that G divides each median in ratio 2:1.
Let ABC be an acute triangle with circumcentre O, orthocentre H and centroid G. Prove that O, G, H are collinear (the Euler line) and that HG = 2 GO.
In triangle ABC let D be the foot of the altitude from A, and let M, N, P be the midpoints of BC, CA, AB. Prove that D, M, N, P lie on a circle (part of the nine-point circle).
Let AB be a diameter of a circle with centre O, and let P be any other point on the circle. Prove that angle APB is a right angle (Thales' theorem).
The incircle of triangle ABC touches sides BC, CA, AB at X, Y, Z. Prove that AY = AZ, BZ = BX and CX = CY, and that AX, BY, CZ are concurrent (at the Gergonne point).
Let H be the orthocentre of acute triangle ABC. Prove that the reflection of H in line BC lies on the circumcircle of ABC.
Given a quadrilateral ABCD, let P, Q, R, S be the midpoints of AB, BC, CD, DA. Prove that PQRS is a parallelogram (Varignon's theorem) whose perimeter equals AC + BD.
Let P be a point outside a circle with centre O. The two tangents from P touch the circle at T1 and T2. Prove that PT1 = PT2 and that OP is the perpendicular bisector of T1T2.
Let P be a point on the circumcircle of triangle ABC. Let X, Y, Z be the feet of the perpendiculars from P to the lines BC, CA, AB. Prove that X, Y, Z are collinear (the Simson line).
In triangle ABC the internal bisectors of angles A, B, C meet the opposite sides; prove that the three angle bisectors are concurrent at the incentre I, which is equidistant from the three sides.
Let ABCD be a parallelogram with D = A + C - B. Prove that the diagonals AC and BD bisect each other, i.e. they share the same midpoint.
Let two circles intersect at points A and B. A line through A meets the circles again at C and D, and a line through B meets them again at E and F. Prove that CE is parallel to DF.
Let A and B be two points on a circle with centre O, and let P be a third point on the major arc. Prove that the central angle AOB is twice the inscribed angle APB (the inscribed angle theorem).
In triangle ABC let M and N be the midpoints of AB and AC. Prove that segment MN is parallel to BC and that MN = BC / 2 (the midline theorem).
Let ABC be a right-angled triangle with the right angle at C, and let M be the midpoint of the hypotenuse AB. Prove that CM = MA = MB, i.e. M is equidistant from all three vertices.
Let AB be a chord of a circle with centre O. Prove that the foot of the perpendicular from O to AB is the midpoint of AB (a perpendicular from the centre bisects a chord).
Prove that the perpendicular bisectors of the three sides of triangle ABC are concurrent at the circumcentre O, which is equidistant from the three vertices.
Let P be an interior point of triangle ABC. Lines AP, BP, CP meet the opposite sides BC, CA, AB at X, Y, Z respectively. Prove that (BX/XC)(CY/YA)(AZ/ZB) = 1 (Ceva's theorem).

Let A be a point on a circle with centre O, and let AB be a chord. Let t be the tangent to the circle at A. Prove that the angle between t and the chord AB equals the inscribed angle ACB for any point C on the major arc (tangent-chord angle).
Two circles intersect at points A and B. Prove that the line AB (the radical axis) is perpendicular to the line joining the centres O1 and O2.

Let ABC be a triangle with circumcentre O and orthocentre H. Let M, N, P be the midpoints of the sides. Prove that the nine-point circle (through M, N, P) has its centre at the midpoint of segment OH.
Let M be the midpoint of side BC of triangle ABC, and let A' be the reflection of A in M. Prove that ABA'C is a parallelogram.

In acute triangle ABC let D, E, F be the feet of the altitudes from A, B, C respectively. The triangle DEF is called the orthic triangle. Prove that the altitudes of ABC are the angle bisectors of triangle DEF.
From a point P outside a circle two secants are drawn. The first meets the circle at A and B, the second at C and D. Prove that PA · PB = PC · PD (the power of a point).
Given three circles in the plane, each pair intersecting in two points, prove that the three radical axes (the lines through each pair's intersection points) are concurrent at the radical centre.

Let P be a point on the circumcircle of triangle ABC with orthocentre H. Prove that the reflections of P in the three side-lines BC, CA, AB are collinear, and that this line (the Steiner line) passes through H.

In triangle ABC let D, E, F be the feet of the altitudes, M, N, P the midpoints of the sides, and X, Y, Z the midpoints of AH, BH, CH where H is the orthocentre. Prove that all nine points lie on one circle (the nine-point circle).
Let O, G, H and N be the circumcentre, centroid, orthocentre and nine-point centre of triangle ABC. Prove that all four lie on one line (the Euler line) and that N is the midpoint of OH.
Let A, B, C, D, E, F be six points on a circle (a hexagon inscribed in a conic). Prove that the three intersection points of opposite sides AB∩DE, BC∩EF, CD∩FA are collinear (Pascal's theorem).
On the sides of triangle ABC, equilateral triangles are erected outward with apexes A1, B1, C1 opposite to A, B, C. Prove that the lines AA1, BB1, CC1 are concurrent (at the first Fermat point).
On the sides of triangle ABC, equilateral triangles are erected outward. Prove that their centres form an equilateral triangle (Napoleon's theorem).
Let H be the orthocentre of triangle ABC and M the midpoint of BC. Prove that the reflection of H in M lies on the circumcircle and is diametrically opposite to A.
Prove that the three altitudes of triangle ABC are concurrent at the orthocentre.
Let P, Q, R be points on sides BC, CA, AB of triangle ABC. Prove that the circumcircles of triangles AQR, BRP and CPQ pass through a common point (the Miquel point).
Let M be the midpoint of a chord PQ of a circle. Two further chords AB and CD are drawn through M. Lines AD and BC meet PQ at X and Y. Prove that M is the midpoint of XY (the butterfly theorem).
Let I be the incentre of triangle ABC and let the bisector of angle A meet the circumcircle again at M. Prove that M is equidistant from B, C and I, i.e. MB = MC = MI (the incentre–excentre lemma).
Let A, B, C be three points on one line and A', B', C' three points on another line. Prove that the three points AB'∩A'B, AC'∩A'C, BC'∩B'C are collinear (Pappus's theorem).

Two triangles ABC and A'B'C' are perspective from a point O (lines AA', BB', CC' concur at O). Prove that the intersection points of corresponding sides AB∩A'B', BC∩B'C', CA∩C'A' are collinear (Desargues's theorem).
Given three circles of different radii with non-collinear centres, prove that the three external centres of similitude (one for each pair) are collinear (Monge's theorem).

A complete quadrilateral is formed by the four lines AB, BC, CD, DA of quadrilateral ABCD, with E = AB∩CD and F = BC∩DA. Prove that the midpoints of the three diagonals AC, BD and EF are collinear (the Newton–Gauss line).
The incircle of triangle ABC touches the sides BC, CA, AB at X, Y, Z. Prove that the lines AX, BY, CZ are concurrent (at the Gergonne point).
For triangle ABC let X, Y, Z be the points where the excircles touch the sides BC, CA, AB respectively. Prove that the lines AX, BY, CZ are concurrent (at the Nagel point).

construction_steps.name: I_A
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(C, A, B), PerpendicularLine(B, AngleBisector(A, B, C)))

construction_steps.name: I_B
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(A, B, C), PerpendicularLine(C, AngleBisector(B, C, A)))

construction_steps.name: I_C
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(B, C, A), PerpendicularLine(A, AngleBisector(C, A, B)))

problem_statement: Let P be a point on the circumcircle of triangle ABC, and let X, Y, Z be the feet of the perpendiculars from P to the sides (the Simson line). Prove that the Simson line bisects the segment PH, where H is the orthocentre.

Let I_A be the centre of the excircle opposite A in triangle ABC, constructed as the intersection of the internal bisector at A with the external bisectors at B and C. Prove that I_A is equidistant from the three side-lines BC, CA, AB.

From an external point P, the two tangents to a circle with centre O touch it at T1 and T2. The line T1T2 is the polar of P. Prove that the polar is perpendicular to OP and that OP passes through the midpoint of T1T2.

Let P be an interior point of triangle ABC. Reflect the cevians AP, BP, CP in the bisectors of angles A, B, C; the reflected lines meet BC, CA, AB at points Ja, Jb, Jc. Prove that AJa, BJb, CJc are concurrent (at the isogonal conjugate of P).
Let the incircle of triangle ABC touch BC at X, and let the A-excircle touch BC at X'. Prove that X and X' are symmetric about the midpoint of BC, i.e. the midpoint of XX' is the midpoint of BC.

construction_steps.name: I_A
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(C, A, B), PerpendicularLine(B, AngleBisector(A, B, C)))

problem_statement: Let I, G and Na be the incentre, centroid and Nagel point of triangle ABC. Prove that these three points are collinear (the Nagel line) with G dividing INa in ratio 1:2.

construction_steps.name: I_A
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(C, A, B), PerpendicularLine(B, AngleBisector(A, B, C)))

construction_steps.name: I_B
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(A, B, C), PerpendicularLine(C, AngleBisector(B, C, A)))

problem_statement: Prove that the nine-point circle of triangle ABC is internally tangent to the incircle (Feuerbach's theorem).
Prove that the nine-point circle of triangle ABC is externally tangent to the excircle opposite A (Feuerbach's theorem for the excircle).

construction_steps.name: I_A
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(C, A, B), PerpendicularLine(B, AngleBisector(A, B, C)))

problem_statement: The incircle of triangle ABC touches BC, CA, AB at X, Y, Z. Prove the equal-tangent relations AY = AZ, BZ = BX and CX = CY.
The incircle of triangle ABC touches the sides at X, Y, Z. Prove that the incentre I is the circumcentre of the contact triangle XYZ, i.e. IX = IY = IZ.

Let the incircle of triangle ABC touch BC at X, let X'' be the point of the incircle diametrically opposite X, and let X' be the point where the A-excircle touches BC. Prove that A, X'' and X' are collinear.

construction_steps.name: I_A
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(C, A, B), PerpendicularLine(B, AngleBisector(A, B, C)))

problem_statement: Let I_A, I_B, I_C be the three excentres of triangle ABC. Prove that the incentre I is the orthocentre of the excentral triangle I_A I_B I_C.

construction_steps.name: I_A
construction_steps.label: I_A
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(C, A, B), PerpendicularLine(B, AngleBisector(A, B, C)))

construction_steps.name: I_B
construction_steps.label: I_B
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(A, B, C), PerpendicularLine(C, AngleBisector(B, C, A)))

construction_steps.name: I_C
construction_steps.label: I_C
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(B, C, A), PerpendicularLine(A, AngleBisector(C, A, B)))

problem_statement: Let I be the incentre and O the circumcentre of triangle ABC, and let V be the reflection of I in O (the Bevan point). Prove that V is the circumcentre of the excentral triangle I_A I_B I_C, i.e. V is equidistant from the three excentres.

construction_steps.name: I_A
construction_steps.label: I_A
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(C, A, B), PerpendicularLine(B, AngleBisector(A, B, C)))

construction_steps.name: I_B
construction_steps.label: I_B
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(A, B, C), PerpendicularLine(C, AngleBisector(B, C, A)))

construction_steps.name: I_C
construction_steps.label: I_C
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(B, C, A), PerpendicularLine(A, AngleBisector(C, A, B)))

problem_statement: Let I_A, I_B, I_C be the excentres of triangle ABC and M_a, M_b, M_c the midpoints of BC, CA, AB. Prove that the lines I_A M_a, I_B M_b, I_C M_c are concurrent (at the Mittenpunkt).

construction_steps.name: I_A
construction_steps.label: I_A
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(C, A, B), PerpendicularLine(B, AngleBisector(A, B, C)))

construction_steps.name: I_B
construction_steps.label: I_B
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(A, B, C), PerpendicularLine(C, AngleBisector(B, C, A)))

construction_steps.name: I_C
construction_steps.label: I_C
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(B, C, A), PerpendicularLine(A, AngleBisector(C, A, B)))

problem_statement: Let I and G be the incentre and centroid of triangle ABC, and let Sp be the Spieker centre (the incentre of the medial triangle). Prove that I, G and Sp are collinear (they lie on the Nagel line).
The incircle of triangle ABC touches CA at Y and AB at Z. Prove that the chord of contact YZ is perpendicular to the line AI joining vertex A to the incentre I.
Let I be the incentre and I_A the A-excentre of triangle ABC. Prove that B and C lie on the circle with diameter II_A, i.e. angle IBI_A = angle ICI_A = 90 degrees.

construction_steps.name: I_A
construction_steps.label: I_A
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(C, A, B), PerpendicularLine(B, AngleBisector(A, B, C)))

problem_statement: Let I_B and I_C be the excentres opposite B and C of triangle ABC. Prove that A, I_B and I_C are collinear (they all lie on the external bisector of angle A).

construction_steps.name: I_B
construction_steps.label: I_B
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(A, B, C), PerpendicularLine(C, AngleBisector(B, C, A)))

construction_steps.name: I_C
construction_steps.label: I_C
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(B, C, A), PerpendicularLine(A, AngleBisector(C, A, B)))

problem_statement: A symmedian of a triangle is the reflection of a median in the corresponding angle bisector. Prove that the three symmedians of triangle ABC are concurrent (at the symmedian / Lemoine point K).

construction_steps.name: symmedian_A
construction_steps.command: Reflect(Line(A, Midpoint(B, C)), AngleBisector(C, A, B))

construction_steps.name: symmedian_B
construction_steps.command: Reflect(Line(B, Midpoint(C, A)), AngleBisector(A, B, C))

construction_steps.name: symmedian_C
construction_steps.command: Reflect(Line(C, Midpoint(A, B)), AngleBisector(B, C, A))

problem_statement: Let the tangents to the circumcircle of triangle ABC at B and C meet at T. Prove that AT is the A-symmedian, i.e. T lies on the reflection of the median AM_a in the bisector of angle A.

construction_steps.name: symmedian_A
construction_steps.command: Reflect(Line(A, Midpoint(B, C)), AngleBisector(C, A, B))

problem_statement: Let the internal and external bisectors of angle A of triangle ABC meet line BC at D and E. Prove that A lies on the circle with diameter DE (the A-Apollonius circle), i.e. its centre is equidistant from A and D.
Two chords AB and CD of a circle meet at an interior point P. Prove that PA · PB = PC · PD (the intersecting chords theorem).
From a point P outside a circle, a tangent touches the circle at T and a secant meets it at A and B. Prove that PT^2 = PA · PB (the tangent–secant relation).
Let ABCD be a cyclic quadrilateral with vertices in order on a circle. Prove Ptolemy's relation AC · BD = AB · CD + AD · BC.

Let the tangent to the circumcircle of triangle ABC at each vertex meet the opposite side-line: at A meeting BC in T_A, at B meeting CA in T_B, at C meeting AB in T_C. Prove that T_A, T_B, T_C are collinear (the Lemoine axis).
Let H be the orthocentre of triangle ABC. Prove that the reflections of H in the three side-lines BC, CA, AB all lie on the circumcircle.

Let H be the orthocentre of triangle ABC and let M_a, M_b, M_c be the midpoints of the sides. Prove that the reflections of H in the three midpoints lie on the circumcircle (and are the antipodes of A, B, C).
Let H be the orthocentre of triangle ABC and P any point on the circumcircle. Prove that the midpoint of HP lies on the nine-point circle.

Let O be the circumcentre of triangle ABC, and let the tangent to the circumcircle at A meet line BC at T. Prove that OA is perpendicular to the tangent line AT (the radius is perpendicular to the tangent).
Let ABCD be a cyclic quadrilateral. Prove that the incentres of triangles ABC, BCD, CDA, DAB form a rectangle (the Japanese theorem for cyclic quadrilaterals).
Let I and I_A be the incentre and A-excentre of triangle ABC, and let M be the second intersection of the bisector of angle A with the circumcircle. Prove that M is the midpoint of segment II_A.
Let N be the nine-point centre of triangle ABC and let D, E, F be the feet of the three altitudes. Prove that N is equidistant from D, E, F (the nine-point circle passes through the altitude feet).

The anticomplementary triangle A'B'C' of triangle ABC has A' = B + C - A, etc. Prove that the orthocentre of A'B'C' coincides with the reflection of the orthocentre H of ABC in the circumcentre O (the de Longchamps point).

construction_steps.name: Hac
construction_steps.label: H'
construction_steps.command: Intersect(PerpendicularLine(Ap, Line(Bp, Cp)), PerpendicularLine(Bp, Line(Ap, Cp)))

problem_statement: Let the incircle of triangle ABC touch the sides at X, Y, Z (the contact triangle), and let O be the circumcentre. Prove that the Euler line of the contact triangle is the line OI, where I is the incentre; equivalently O, I and the orthocentre of XYZ are collinear.

construction_steps.name: Hc
construction_steps.label: H_c
construction_steps.command: Intersect(PerpendicularLine(X, Line(Y, Z)), PerpendicularLine(Y, Line(X, Z)))

problem_statement: Let A'B'C' be the anticomplementary triangle of ABC, with A' = B + C - A. Prove that ABC is the medial triangle of A'B'C', so that the centroid G is the midpoint of each segment AA'.
Let M be the midpoint of arc BC not containing A on the circumcircle of triangle ABC. Prove that M is the centre of a circle passing through B, C, the incentre I and the A-excentre I_A.
Prove that in triangle ABC the bisector of angle A and the perpendicular bisector of side BC meet on the circumcircle, at the midpoint of arc BC.
Let P and P' be two diametrically opposite points on the circumcircle of triangle ABC. Prove that the Simson lines of P and P' are perpendicular to each other.
Let H be the orthocentre of triangle ABC. Prove that the circle through A, B and H has the same radius as the circumcircle of ABC.

Let the internal and external bisectors of angle A of triangle ABC meet the circumcircle again at M and M'. Prove that M and M' are diametrically opposite (MM' is a diameter), hence O is the midpoint of MM'.
Prove that the reflection of vertex A in the perpendicular bisector of BC lies on the circumcircle of triangle ABC (it is the point that swaps B and C).
In triangle ABC let E and F be the feet of the altitudes from B and C, and let H be the orthocentre. Prove that A, E, H, F are concyclic (they lie on the circle with diameter AH).
Three circles each pass through the same two points A and B. Prove that their three centres are collinear (they lie on the perpendicular bisector of AB).

A cyclic quadrilateral ABCD has perpendicular diagonals meeting at E. Prove that the line through E perpendicular to side BC passes through the midpoint of the opposite side AD (Brahmagupta's theorem).
The diagonals AC and BD of a cyclic quadrilateral ABCD meet at P. Prove that PA · PC = PB · PD.
A transversal line meets the side-lines BC, CA, AB of triangle ABC at D, E, F respectively. Prove Menelaus's relation (AF·BD·CE) = (FB·DC·EA).
In a trapezoid ABCD with AB parallel to CD, let P be the intersection of the diagonals and Q the intersection of the legs AD and BC. Prove that P, Q and the midpoints of AB and CD are collinear.

For a quadrilateral ABCD, prove that the midpoint of the segment joining the midpoints of the diagonals AC and BD coincides with the centre of the Varignon parallelogram (the midpoint of the segment joining the midpoints of opposite sides AB and CD).
Let M be the midpoint of side BC of triangle ABC. Prove that the median AM divides the triangle into two triangles ABM and ACM of equal area.
Let G be the centroid of triangle ABC. Prove that the three triangles GBC, GCA, GAB have equal area (each one third of the area of ABC).

Given three circles of distinct radii, prove that the external centre of similitude of one pair and the internal centres of similitude of the other two pairs are collinear (the second theorem of d'Alembert / Monge).
For a convex quadrilateral ABCD, the bisectors of consecutive interior angles meet in four points. Prove that these four points are concyclic.

construction_steps.name: K1
construction_steps.label: K₁
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(D, A, B), AngleBisector(A, B, C))

construction_steps.name: K2
construction_steps.label: K₂
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(A, B, C), AngleBisector(B, C, D))

construction_steps.name: K3
construction_steps.label: K₃
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(B, C, D), AngleBisector(C, D, A))

construction_steps.name: K4
construction_steps.label: K₄
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(C, D, A), AngleBisector(D, A, B))

problem_statement: Two circles intersect at A and B. Prove that the line of centres O1O2 is the perpendicular bisector of the common chord AB.
Prove that the medial triangle (formed by the midpoints of the sides) of triangle ABC has the same centroid as ABC.

Given four points A, B, C, D, let P be the intersection of lines AB and CD. Prove that the second intersection X of the circumcircles of triangles PAC and PBD is the centre of the spiral similarity that maps A to C and B to D (so XC·XB = XD·XA).
A quadrilateral ABCD is circumscribed about a circle (each side is tangent to it). Prove Pitot's theorem: AB + CD = BC + DA.
A quadrilateral ABCD is circumscribed about a circle with centre O. Prove Newton's theorem: O lies on the line joining the midpoints of the two diagonals AC and BD.
A hexagon ABCDEF is circumscribed about a circle. Prove Brianchon's theorem: the three main diagonals AD, BE, CF are concurrent.
On the sides of an arbitrary quadrilateral ABCD, squares are erected externally with centres P, Q, R, S. Prove Van Aubel's theorem: the segments PR and QS are equal in length and perpendicular.
Let the internal bisector of angle A of triangle ABC meet BC at D. Prove the angle bisector theorem BD/DC = AB/AC, i.e. BD·AC = DC·AB.
On the sides of triangle ABC, equilateral triangles are erected inward, with centres N_a, N_b, N_c. Prove that N_a N_b N_c is equilateral (the inner Napoleon triangle).

Let I_A, I_B, I_C be the excentres of triangle ABC. Prove that the midpoints of the sides of the excentral triangle lie on the circumcircle of ABC (the circumcircle is the nine-point circle of the excentral triangle).

construction_steps.name: I_A
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(C, A, B), PerpendicularLine(B, AngleBisector(A, B, C)))

construction_steps.name: I_B
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(A, B, C), PerpendicularLine(C, AngleBisector(B, C, A)))

construction_steps.name: I_C
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(B, C, A), PerpendicularLine(A, AngleBisector(C, A, B)))

problem_statement: Through the incentre I of triangle ABC draw the line parallel to BC, meeting AB at P and AC at Q. Prove that PQ = BP + CQ.

Let M_A, M_B, M_C be the midpoints of arcs BC, CA, AB (not containing the opposite vertices) of the circumcircle of triangle ABC. Prove that the incentre I of ABC is the orthocentre of triangle M_A M_B M_C.
Let P be a point on side AB of triangle ABC, and let the line through P parallel to BC meet AC at Q. Prove the basic proportionality (Thales) relation AP/PB = AQ/QC, i.e. AP·QC = PB·AQ.
Let ABC be an equilateral triangle and P a point on the arc BC of its circumcircle not containing A. Prove that PA = PB + PC.
Let the internal and external bisectors of angle A of triangle ABC meet line BC at D and E. Prove that D and E divide BC harmonically, i.e. BD/DC = BE/EC (so BD·EC = DC·BE).
Let the internal and external bisectors of angle A of triangle ABC meet BC at D and E, and let M be the midpoint of DE. Prove the Apollonius relation MD^2 = MB · MC.

Prove that the circumcentre O and the orthocentre H of triangle ABC are isogonal conjugates, by showing that the reflection of line AO in the bisector of angle A is the altitude from A (it is perpendicular to BC).

In acute triangle ABC let D, E, F be the feet of the altitudes. Prove that the three points where the sides of the orthic triangle DEF meet the corresponding sides of ABC are collinear (the orthic axis).

Let the tangents to the circumcircle of triangle ABC at A, B, C form the tangential triangle with vertices TA (tangents at B,C), TB (tangents at C,A), TC (tangents at A,B). Prove that ATA, BTB, CTC are concurrent (at the symmedian point).
Let M be the midpoint of side BC of triangle ABC. Prove the median-length (Apollonius) theorem AB^2 + AC^2 = 2 AM^2 + 2 BM^2.
In a parallelogram ABCD prove the parallelogram law: the sum of the squares of the two diagonals equals twice the sum of the squares of two adjacent sides, AC^2 + BD^2 = 2 AB^2 + 2 AD^2.
Let ABCD be a rectangle and P any point in the plane. Prove the British flag theorem: PA^2 + PC^2 = PB^2 + PD^2.

Three circles of equal radius pass through a common point H. Let A, B, C be their second pairwise intersection points. Prove Johnson's theorem: the circumcircle of ABC has the same radius as the three given circles.
Let O and H be the circumcentre and orthocentre of triangle ABC, and M the midpoint of BC. Prove the Euler vector relation AH = 2·OM (equivalently H = A + 2(M - O)).
Let O and H be the circumcentre and orthocentre of triangle ABC. Taking O as origin, prove the vector identity OH = OA + OB + OC, i.e. H = A + B + C - 2O.
Let O, G and H be the circumcentre, centroid and orthocentre of triangle ABC. Prove that G lies on segment OH with OG : GH = 1 : 2, i.e. G = O + (H - O)/3.
Two circles intersect at A and B. Let P be a point on line AB outside both circles. Prove that the tangent lengths from P to the two circles are equal (P has equal power to both circles).
Let I_A be the A-excentre of triangle ABC, touching line AB at Tc and line AC at Tb. Prove that the tangent lengths AT_b and AT_c are equal (both equal the semiperimeter s).

construction_steps.name: I_A
construction_steps.label: I_A
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(C, A, B), PerpendicularLine(B, AngleBisector(A, B, C)))

problem_statement: Let K be the symmedian (Lemoine) point of triangle ABC, and let Ka, Kb, Kc be the feet of the perpendiculars from K to the sides. Prove that K is the centroid of its own pedal triangle Ka Kb Kc.

construction_steps.name: symmedian_A
construction_steps.command: Reflect(Line(A, Midpoint(B, C)), AngleBisector(C, A, B))

construction_steps.name: symmedian_B
construction_steps.command: Reflect(Line(B, Midpoint(C, A)), AngleBisector(A, B, C))

problem_statement: Let F be the first Fermat point of triangle ABC (each side subtends 120 degrees there). Prove that angle AFB = angle BFC = angle CFA = 120 degrees.
Let A and B be fixed points on a circle, and let P and Q be two points on the same arc. Prove that the inscribed angles APB and AQB subtending the chord AB are equal.
From an external point P, the two tangents to a circle with centre O touch it at T1 and T2. Prove that PO bisects the angle T1PT2, i.e. angle T1PO = angle T2PO.
Let ABCD be a cyclic quadrilateral. Prove that the exterior angle at C (between side CB and the extension of side DC) equals the interior angle at the opposite vertex A.
In triangle ABC with AB = AC (constructed so that C is the rotation of B about A), prove that the base angles are equal: angle ABC = angle ACB.
Prove that the diagonals of a rectangle are equal in length.
Construct a rhombus ABCD with AD obtained by rotating B about A (so AB = AD) and C = B + D - A. Prove that the diagonals AC and BD are perpendicular.
Prove that the diagonals of an isosceles trapezoid (AB parallel to CD with AD = BC) are equal in length.
Let G be the centroid of triangle ABC and let a line through G meet AB at P and AC at Q. Prove the reciprocal relation AB/AP + AC/AQ = 3.

Let P be a point on the circumcircle of triangle ABC. Reflect the cevians AP, BP, CP in the bisectors of the respective angles; let the reflected lines meet the opposite sides at Ja, Jb, Jc. Prove that AJa, BJb, CJc are parallel (the isogonal conjugate of a point on the circumcircle is at infinity).

Prove that the pedal triangle of the incentre I of triangle ABC (the feet of the perpendiculars from I to the sides) is the contact triangle, i.e. the feet coincide with the incircle's points of tangency.

Prove that the pedal triangle of the circumcentre O of triangle ABC (the feet of the perpendiculars from O to the sides) is the medial triangle, i.e. each foot is the midpoint of the corresponding side.
A complete quadrilateral is formed by four lines, giving rise to four triangles. Prove that the orthocentres of these four triangles are collinear (the Steiner line of the complete quadrilateral).

construction_steps.name: H1
construction_steps.label: H₁
construction_steps.command: Intersect(PerpendicularLine(B, Line(C, E)), PerpendicularLine(C, Line(B, E)))

construction_steps.name: H2
construction_steps.label: H₂
construction_steps.command: Intersect(PerpendicularLine(A, Line(B, F)), PerpendicularLine(B, Line(A, F)))

construction_steps.name: H3
construction_steps.label: H₃
construction_steps.command: Intersect(PerpendicularLine(A, Line(D, E)), PerpendicularLine(D, Line(A, E)))

construction_steps.name: H4
construction_steps.label: H₄
construction_steps.command: Intersect(PerpendicularLine(C, Line(D, F)), PerpendicularLine(D, Line(C, F)))

problem_statement: In acute triangle ABC let D, E, F be the feet of the altitudes. From each foot drop perpendiculars to the other two sides, producing six points. Prove that these six points are concyclic (the Taylor circle).

Let O and H be the circumcentre and orthocentre of triangle ABC, and let O' be the reflection of O in line BC. Prove that O' is the circumcentre of triangle BHC, i.e. O' is equidistant from B, H and C.
Let M_A be the midpoint of the arc BC not containing A of the circumcircle of triangle ABC. Prove that the tangent to the circumcircle at M_A is parallel to BC.
Let O be the circumcentre and N the nine-point centre of triangle ABC, with M_a the midpoint of BC. Prove that the nine-point circle has radius half the circumradius, i.e. NM_a = OA / 2.
Prove that the orthocentre of the medial triangle of ABC coincides with the circumcentre O of ABC.

construction_steps.name: Hm
construction_steps.label: H_m
construction_steps.command: Intersect(PerpendicularLine(Ma, Line(Mb, Mc)), PerpendicularLine(Mb, Line(Ma, Mc)))

problem_statement: Let I and O be the incentre and circumcentre of triangle ABC, V the reflection of I in O (the Bevan point), and I_A the A-excentre. Prove that the Bevan circle (circumcircle of the excentral triangle) has radius 2R, i.e. VI_A equals the diameter AA' of the circumcircle.

construction_steps.name: I_A
construction_steps.label: I_A
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(C, A, B), PerpendicularLine(B, AngleBisector(A, B, C)))

problem_statement: In a trapezoid ABCD with AB parallel to CD, let M and N be the midpoints of the legs AD and BC. Prove that the midsegment MN is parallel to the bases and MN = (AB + CD)/2.
In a trapezoid ABCD with AB parallel to CD, prove that the segment joining the midpoints of the two diagonals AC and BD is parallel to the bases.
In an isosceles triangle with AB = AC, prove that the bisectors of the two base angles B and C have equal length (the easy direction of the Steiner–Lehmus theorem).
Let ABC be a right-angled triangle with the right angle at C. Prove that its circumcentre is the midpoint of the hypotenuse AB.
From an external point P, a tangent to a circle with centre O touches it at T. Prove that triangle OTP is right-angled at T, i.e. OP^2 = OT^2 + PT^2.
Construct a kite ABCD whose axis of symmetry is the diagonal AC, with B and D reflections of each other in line AC. Prove that the diagonals AC and BD are perpendicular.

Let ABC be a triangle inscribed in a circle with centre O, and let A' be the point diametrically opposite A. Prove that BA'C... that A'B is perpendicular to AB (angle ABA' is a right angle, since AA' is a diameter).

Let Ω be the first Brocard point of triangle ABC, defined as the second intersection of the circle through A,B tangent to BC at B with the circle through B,C tangent to CA at C. Prove that angle ΩAB = angle ΩBC = angle ΩCA.
